results.possibleIdentity: Charles Lutner was a studio glass artist and craftsman active during the late 1960s and 1970s. He is noted for his work in hand-blown art glass, including vases, paperweights, and goblets, which often feature swirl patterns and bubble accents. In 1969, while based in Colorado Springs, Colorado, he was selected to design and produce the Governor's Award for Excellence in the Arts. By the mid-1970s, he was operating from Fromberg, Montana. His work was featured in the prestigious 'Crafts Multiples' exhibition at the Renwick Gallery of the Smithsonian Institution's National Collection of Fine Arts, which opened on July 4, 1975. This exhibition highlighted American production craftsmen and featured his hand-blown 'New Jersey' goblets. His work from this period is held in the records and archives of the Smithsonian Institution. While no direct membership in Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is explicitly documented in available records, his participation in Smithsonian exhibitions that explored the intersection of technical production and artistic design aligned with the contemporary interests of the E.A.T. movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Don Hill is a Canadian sound artist, broadcaster, and storyteller based in Alberta. He has been active in interdisciplinary arts and media since the early 1970s (with archival records dating back to 1973). Hill is known for his long-standing commitment to collaborations between artists, scientists, and engineers—a goal shared by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. His work includes interactive media installations such as "Story Trees," which transports radio concepts into a digital, interactive format, and a 2023 gallery project that transformed urban construction noise into an industrial symphony controlled by user gestures. Hill has served as a thought leader at the Banff Centre and has extensively chronicled the legacy of neuroscientist Michael Persinger, specifically his work with the "God Helmet" (an experiment in neurotheology and the sense of presence). He often advocates for "inter-disciplinary collaboration" and has produced programs for the CBC and Access TV on topics ranging from folklore to biotechnology.

Donald R. Hill (1922–1994) was a British engineer and preeminent historian of Islamic technology whose work is frequently cited in the context of art, history, and technology. He became particularly relevant to the Art and Technology movement of the 1960s and 1970s through his definitive 1974 translation of Al-Jazari's "The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices." This medieval text described sophisticated automata, water clocks, and "fine technology" that integrated engineering with artistic craftsmanship. Hill's scholarly work validated the functionality of historical automata, aligning with the interests of movements like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to merge aesthetic goals with scientific and technical rigor. He served in the Royal Engineers and obtained his PhD in 1970, with his work subsequently appearing in significant Art and Technology bibliographies alongside the 9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering archives.

Don Hill is an Australian contemporary artist, photographer, and writer who was active during the 1970s and beyond. He was tutored in the early 1970s by artists such as Frank DeSilva, Arthur Evan Read, and Mervyn Moriarty. He is associated with the contemporary art movement known as "The Recyclers," which focuses on environmental issues and uses discarded debris and objects to create artworks. His practice is multidisciplinary and often involves collaborations with other environmental artists like John Dahlsen. His work is visually descriptive and frequently inspired by his travels in Australia and New Zealand, exploring themes of landscape and nature.
